front: What is the precise statement of the Heine-Borel Theorem?
back: A subset K of ℝⁿ is compact (with the Euclidean metric) if and only if it is closed and bounded.

front: Does the Heine-Borel Theorem hold in all metric spaces?
back: No. The Heine-Borel Theorem is a special property of ℝⁿ (with the Euclidean metric) and a few other specific spaces. In general metric spaces, closed and bounded does not necessarily imply compact.

front: What is the significance of the Heine-Borel Theorem in real analysis?
back: It provides a simple and practical characterization of compactness in ℝⁿ, allowing us to identify compact sets (which have very useful properties like every sequence having a convergent subsequence) just by checking if they are closed and bounded.

front: Give an example of a metric space where a set is closed and bounded but NOT compact.
back: The set ℕ (the natural numbers) in the metric space (ℝ, discrete metric) is closed and bounded (all points are distance 1 apart), but it is not compact. The open cover consisting of singleton sets {n} for each n ∈ ℕ has no finite subcover.

front: How does the Heine-Borel Theorem connect the concepts of compactness, closedness, and boundedness?
back: In ℝⁿ, it shows that these three properties are equivalent for a set: K is compact ⇔ K is closed ⇔ K is closed and bounded. This is a powerful link between a topological property (compactness/closedness) and a metric property (boundedness).
